attributes.stem: A 21-year-old woman presents with increasing deafness. Over the last two months she has noticed worsening of her hearing, particularly when listening to the radio. Her medical history includes a number of childhood ear infections. On examination there is scarring on both ear drums with visible amorphous debris in the left pars flaccida.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of the hearing loss?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a likely, unilateral conductive hearing loss and evidence of amorphous debris in the pars flaccida. As such, a cholesteatoma should be considered until proven otherwise. Her history suggests multiple ear infections with likely perforation (evident as scaring on the ear drums). A perforation may have provided a breach in the tympanic membrane which allowed the keratin to enter the middle ear from the external ear.

A cholesteatoma is essentially a growth of desquamated, stratified, squamous epithelium within the middle ear canal. These lesions may occur either as primary or secondary to tympanic membrane perforation (as mentioned above). Left untreated, they may erode the cerebral sinuses, or even the inner ear and may result in sepsis, brain abscess, sensorineural hearing loss, or vertigo (to name a few). Cholesteatoma may be asymptomatic, but features may include hearing loss (conductive), dizziness, and/or otorrhea.

In terms for the incorrect answers above:

  •  Otitis externa: This condition is most common between the ages of 5 and 9 and affects up to 10% of people during their lifetime. It typically develops due to trauma, paired with bacterial overgrowth promoted by exposure to water—such as swimming, showering, or hot, humid conditions. Frequently identified symptoms are pain, pruritic ears with discharge, and hearing loss. Otoscopic exam normally appears oedematous and erythematous in the external part of the ear canal. Despite her recurrent childhood infections, the examination findings are inconsistent with otitis externa as it does not typically involve the pars flaccida (which is the flaccid portion of the tympanic membrane).

  • Otosclerosis: Typifies a bony overgrowth that involves the footplate of the stapes which progressively causing fixation of the stapes. This typically affects 20–40-year old’s and causes a conductive hearing loss with tinnitus, vertigo (in some). Patients may report better hearing in noisy environments (Paracusis willisii) and the Schwartze sign may be evident on otoscopic exam. This condition is not associated with amorphous debris in the left pars flaccida.

  • Presbycusis: Also known as age-related hearing loss which is accompanied by a hallmark “progressive, symmetric loss of high-frequency hearing” in elderly individuals. Common issues faced by these individuals include tinnitus, an inability to hear speech in noisy areas, and the inability to hear high-pitched voices or noises. Our patient is 21-years-old and unlikely to have age-related­ hearing loss.

  • Acoustic neuroma: Of tumours that cause sensorineural hearing loss, the most common is an acoustic neuroma; originating from the vestibular portion of cranial nerve eight. Typical complaints include a unilateral or asymmetric or sensorineural hearing loss, as well as unilateral tinnitus, dizziness, and headaches. The test of choice is an MRI, usually ordered in a patient with unexplained unilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Our patient likely has conductive hearing loss as evident by her inability to hear the radio which makes an acoustic neuroma unlikely.
learningPoints: Cholesteatoma is a keratinised mass in the middle ear, that may occur as primary lesion or secondary to tympanic membrane perforation.
references: Chang P. Cholesteatoma. In: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP) [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/a13da39c-2308-451c-8d29-858af3c0382f/attachment.aspx
Weber, PC. Etiology of hearing loss in adults [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021 [cited 16 August 2021]. Available from: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/etiology-of-hearing-loss-in-adults>

Lustig, LR. Limb, CJ. Chronic otitis media, cholesteatoma, and mastoiditis in adults [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021 [cited 16 August 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/chronic-otitis-media-cholesteatoma-and-mastoiditis-in-adults>

Goguen, LA,. External otitis: Pathogenesis, clinical features, and diagnosis [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021 [cited 16 August 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/external-otitis-pathogenesis-clinical-features-and-diagnosis>
